data.title: IWMI: A Different Take On Income Investing
data.description:
NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI): A Different Take on Income Investing

I rate the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (IWMI) as a Buy. The main reason is simple. It offers something that’s still hard to find in the options income ETF space: real exposure to small-cap stocks, paired with a strategy that generates income without completely giving up growth potential.

Most high-income ETFs stick to large-cap indexes like the S&P 500 or Nasdaq-100. Many funds end up looking like slight variations of the same idea, especially with mega-cap stocks dominating those indexes with the MAG 7 representing over 30% of the S&P 500. IWMI stands out because it focuses on the Russell 2000, where volatility runs higher and option premiums tend to be richer. That creates a stronger foundation for income while adding exposure to a part of the market that often gets overlooked.

This isn’t only about yield. IWMI gives investors a way to own small caps while getting paid for the added volatility. For anyone trying to boost income without piling even more into mega-cap-heavy strategies, that’s a meaningful difference.

Why IWMI Stands Out

The appeal starts with small caps themselves. They tend to move more, react more to economic shifts, and go through sharper cycles than large caps. In a traditional buy-and-hold portfolio, those traits can feel like a drawback. In an options-based strategy, they can be an advantage.

Higher volatility leads to higher option premiums. That’s the engine behind IWMI’s income. Instead of relying heavily on portfolio turnover or aggressive payout policies, the fund benefits from a stronger underlying environment for generating income.

There’s also a diversification angle. Many investors already have heavy exposure to large-cap stocks, especially in tech. Adding another large-cap income ETF increases yield, but it doesn’t change much else. IWMI adds both income and a different type of market exposure, which helps balance a portfolio.

Russell 2000 top 10 holdings:

The Role of Active Management

IWMI and NEOS funds don't follow a rigid covered call strategy that automatically sells away most of the upside. Instead, NEOS Investments runs an actively managed options strategy where key levers, like how much of the portfolio is overwritten, strike selection, and trade timing, are adjusted continuously. Based on NEOS’s own materials, the fund typically does not maintain a constant 100% overwrite. Coverage often moves within a range (commonly around 50% to 100%) depending on market conditions, which allows part of the portfolio to remain exposed to upside price appreciation as well.

Strike selection is also dynamic. Rather than consistently selling at-the-money calls, NEOS often uses out-of-the-money calls to leave room for price appreciation. In stronger markets, they can push strikes further out and reduce overwrite levels, which lowers immediate income but preserves more upside and protects long term NAV. In flatter or more volatile conditions, they can increase coverage and bring strikes closer to the current price to collect higher premiums and potentially protect from downside price movement.

Timing plays a role as well. Instead of writing options on a fixed monthly schedule, the strategy allows for opportunistic execution, particularly during periods of elevated volatility when premiums are more attractive. This matters even more given IWMI’s exposure to the Russell 2000 Index, where volatility tends to be structurally higher, creating a stronger premium environment to work with.

Looking into the prospectus we see that IWMI's call writing program is not designed to replicate a traditional covered call index outcome. The fund uses index options that are tied to the Russell 2000 and combines this with a clever tax managed structure, which allows the fund to retain more flexibility in how gains are going to be realized and distributed. This matters in total return because it avoids forcing the fund into systematically capping the upside at the same level every options cycle.

This means that IWMI's upside capture is not set in stone, its variable. When overwrite levels are lower and strikes are further out-of-the-money, a huge portion of the portfolio will participate in a rally. This creates a very different return profile compared to a fixed traditional strategy that writes calls on 100% of the underlying. This causes upside to be limited regardless of current market conditions.

That flexibility shows up in outcomes. Traditional Buy-Write covered call funds tend to perform best in flat markets but lag when stocks rise quickly because their upside is capped early. IWMI aims for a middle ground. It collects solid income while still leaving room to benefit from market gains, especially since a portion of the portfolio often remains uncapped.

This is the key point when comparing IWMI to the plain Russell 2000 underlying. A non-income ETF will outperform in a sharp bull market because it will capture 100% of the upside. IWMI is not designed to win in that exact scenario, but it is built to generate a higher total return across multiple market environments. In a range-bound, volatile market, or in markets that are gradually trending higher. In those conditions, the combination of option income and partial upside participation can close the gap and, in some cases, outperform on a total return basis with significantly less BETA and volatility to a portfolio.

The trade-off is clear: by investing in IWMI you will give up some upside in strong rallies in exchange for consistent income and less volatility. The reason to buy IWMI is not that it will always beat the underlying Russell 2000, but it can produce a more balanced outcome when returns are driven as much by volatility and income as price appreciation alone. Most often, IWMI and the Russell 2000 can be bought in pairs, so you can get the best of both worlds.

Why It’s a Buy Right Now

The Buy rating comes down to both opportunity and positioning.

Small caps still look appealing compared to crowded large-cap trades. Valuations have often been more reasonable, and if market leadership starts to broaden, the Russell 2000 has room to move.

At the same time, IWMI doesn’t rely only on price gains. The income component helps carry returns, even if the market moves sideways or climbs slowly. That makes holding the fund easier compared to a pure small-cap ETF, where performance depends heavily on price appreciation.

It also fills a clear role in an income-focused portfolio. This isn’t a core holding, but it works well as way for investors who want higher yield to diversify from big tech.

This is why I believe IWMI's structure aligns with the current setup. If small caps continue to trade with elevated volatility rather than a straight upward trend, IWMI will continue to monetize that volatility and keep turning it into options premiums while capturing some of that upside. This combination increases the probability of competitive total returns versus a passive alternative that would depend on market direction.

Total return comparison between IWMI, SPY, the NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI), which has a somewhat similar strategy but references QQQ, and the NEOS S&P 500 High Income ETF (SPYI), which has a more flexible options strategy and references the SP500.

Recent total return data shows IWMI outperforming these peers, which reinforces the strength of its structure in the current environment. Compared to large-cap income ETFs like QQQI and SPYI, IWMI benefits from higher small-cap volatility, which supports richer option premiums, and, combined with partial upside participation, has allowed it to outperform across recent periods. Given the current market conditions, where small caps are supported by higher volatility and less crowded positioning, IWMI provides a compelling way to gain exposure while reducing reliance on big tech-heavy portfolios.

The Risks to Watch

Like most high-income ETFs, the biggest concern is NAV (Net asset value) erosion.

If the payouts aren’t supported by the strategy, investors can end up collecting income while the fund’s value slowly declines. That’s something to monitor closely. With IWMI, small-cap volatility adds another layer. It helps generate income, but it also means sharper drawdowns when markets turn.

There’s also the trade-off with upside. Even with active management, an options strategy won’t capture the full benefit of a strong rally. If small caps surge, IWMI should participate, but not to the same extent as a pure index fund.

Finally, the track record is still limited. The idea makes sense, but it hasn’t been tested over a full market cycle yet.

Bottom Line

IWMI brings together three things that are hard to find in one fund: strong income, small-cap exposure, and a flexible options strategy that still leaves room for growth.

It isn’t risk-free, and it shouldn’t replace core equity holdings. Still, for investors looking to increase income while stepping away from the usual large-cap-heavy strategies, IWMI offers a compelling alternative. The same volatility that makes small caps risky also drives the opportunity here.

data.title: SCHD: Why I'm Choosing The S&P 500 Over This Dividend Favorite
data.description:
The Schwab US Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) is an instrument that ended up becoming a benchmark. I would even say synonymous with dividend investing. It created this status of delivering income — a high quality income.

And of course, there are solid reasons behind this, it is not just hype, after all, SCHD’s track record is quite long. There is really a very interesting methodology behind the ETF, good reasons to invest, and one of them is a solid expense ratio of 0.06%. Not to mention the reasonable 3.4% dividend yield and 14 years of dividend growth, much better than peers (see below).

But at the same time, I do not think it is an obvious buy in the current scenario, especially if we take into account the opportunity cost.

SCHD And The “Forced” Diversification

In my view, we should compare SCHD with the ‘market’, that is, S&P500 (SPY) (IVV) and Nasdaq (QQQ).

This is because even though it is clearly possible to have S&P500, Nasdaq and SCHD in the same portfolio, the investor’s capital is fundamentally a scarce asset, so if you just increased your position in SCHD, it is because you did not increase it in the S&P500 and vice versa.

Considering that most investors nowadays hold passive ETFs that invest in the mentioned indexes, we have a problem of high concentration. High concentration in what? First, there is a very high concentration in technology. More specifically, if you buy SPY today, you are buying 33% in technology (even after the magnificent seven went through a strong correction). But more than that, you are buying more than a third of the portfolio in 10 stocks, such as NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA), Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Microsoft Corporation (MSFT).

I do not see a problem in investing in these companies, they are excellent compounders, with good prospects and a good valuation. But the problem is that there is a high dependence on these names working out. And that is where SCHD acts as a diversifier that “forces” you to hold stocks that you would not hold, acting almost like a hedge.

A clear example that happened recently was with the energy sector. The two largest positions in SCHD currently are Chevron Corporation (CVX) and Conoco Phillips (COP), two stocks that year to date delivered gains of ~20%. Even though they are the two main stocks, there is no strong dependence on these two names, because their weights are 4.58% and 4.3% respectively, and in the next rebalance, SCHD will sell part of these positions because no stock can exceed 4%.

You might argue that the S&P500 also benefited from this energy trend, and that is true, but while the S&P500 has ~4% of its holdings in energy, SCHD has almost 17%. The part about reducing to 4% also catches my attention because it ends up being a hedge against overvalued stocks. A hypothetical example, if an energy stock that used to trade at 15x obtained a re-rating to 30x, SCHD will benefit, but as soon as the rebalance happens, it will reduce the position at the valuation “peak”. If there is a de-rating afterward, SCHD acted as a hedge. If market optimism really materializes and there is massive cash flow generation, high dividend yield, great, SCHD will also capture part of these gains.

Beyond that, energy is not even the best example in my view. Still talking about sectors, SCHD has 19% in healthcare, 18% in consumer defensive, 8.5% in industrials, 7% in communications, which are sectors that are often ignored or have almost irrelevant weight in most portfolios (including my own), so having this diversification in these sectors ends up being a good hedge when there is a rotation like we saw recently (that’s why it outperformed the S&P500 and Nasdaq YTD), a market less optimistic about growth stocks, and more optimistic with hard assets + huge cash flow companies.

SCHD’s qualitative filter is also very well designed and ends up neutralizing opinions we might have about a stock. For instance, we only have stocks with adequate market cap and volume, after that, only companies that have a reliable dividend (+10 years consecutively), and after that there are still filters for debt, return on equity and dividend growth rate. This ends up making the ETF hold stocks such as Verizon Communications Inc. (VZ), which is a cash cow, because the numbers are good and fit this filter, even if the market is more pessimistic and afraid of disruption.

As I discussed in my recent Verizon article (check it HERE), I would not buy Verizon for the portfolio currently. But 4% as is SCHD’s weight? Ok, it’s acceptable, it is good diversification, SCHD will collect dividends, may capture upside, and if it starts to go wrong (stops paying dividends, has weak ROE and debt metrics), SCHD reduces the position or sells everything.

And the list goes further, at this moment, I would also not like to have a strong position in The Coca-Cola Company (KO) or PepsiCo, Inc. (PEP), but having a little in each just to receive dividends and have this automatic ETF rebalance seems good enough.

It is also worth mentioning that, as I have already said in other articles, I am not an income-oriented investor. But even so, I need to say that there is value in selecting stocks based on dividends, but the relationship is somewhat counterintuitive. I disagree with the idea that a company can be "inherently good" just because it pays dividends. However, it is a truth that great companies are the ones capable of sustaining cash flow and dividends. That is why the filter works. Only companies that are cash flow rich, have controlled debt, can sustain a dividend. More than that, only companies that have moats, such as pricing power, can sustain dividend increases over long periods.

So overall, I think SCHD does play an important role. The expense ratio is low, diversification is high, the methodology is good, and it ends up protecting the investor from concentration risk (for example, having 10% in KO stock just because of the dividend and it suffering a drawdown due to a valuation problem or something like that).

The Reason I Do Not Want To Own SCHD (For Now)

In the long term, as you could see in the section above, I like SCHD, it is a good instrument to compose a portfolio.

But I do not think it is the best moment to buy SCHD.

This is because the positive aspect I mentioned about diversification and “forcing” you to hold several stocks ends up being a double-edged sword. For example, in a moment where the market is dismissing cash cows, does not care about mature companies, and only wants growth, holding SCHD ends up protecting you from a de-rating in tech stocks.

But while this forced diversification acts as a safety net during these corrections, it also creates a significant drag during periods of high-tech growth, sustained tech advancement, or just some major bullish sentiment. Think about it. The S&P500 and Nasdaq right now are fueled with some secular growth stocks, based on AI, cloud computing, and similars. But by mandate, SCHD excludes these high growth stocks because they lack the 10y dividend track record, I know it's fair, but this characteristic limits your upside. I mean, in a market that rewards innovation and scalability (think not only in the next year, but in the next 5), SCHD's heavy tilt towards dividend, value and mature companies, forces you to own these slow growth behemoths.

But after the de-rating has already happened, which I believe is the case, it seems to make much more sense to be more optimistic with indexes such as the S&P500 and Nasdaq than with an ETF like this. I am not saying it does not make sense to own SCHD, but rather that between buying the index or doing stock picking and buying SCHD, I prefer to buy other things.

I will classify this reason as opportunity cost.

If I think SCHD will not be able to outperform in the next quarters/years, I do not think it makes sense to assign a buy rating to it.

The reason I do not think SCHD will outperform is simple. It is a mix between valuation and growth.

As I mentioned in the previous section, the ETF from Charles Schwab ends up holding many mature companies. Oil companies that already went through a re-rating and are already pricing higher oil prices. And worse, several consumer staples companies that in my view still need to prove they can deliver healthy shareholder value at these prices.

Among the examples, I will mention PepsiCo, The Procter & Gamble Company (PG) and Coca-Cola. Having Verizon, which is a cash cow with a single digit P/E and high dividend yield potential is one thing. Holding these other three companies I mentioned is a different story.

And the reason is that I think all three are a reflection of a market that is mispricing consumer staples. Note that for Coca-Cola, PepsiCo and Procter & Gamble, the consensus forward revenue growth is below low single digits. PG for example, the market expects it to grow 1.9%. Even with slight margin expansion, it is still not possible to be that optimistic about these companies’ bottom line growth in the next year.

This growth would not be a problem at all if the market were pricing these companies as mature consumer staples. But that is not the case. They are pricing these companies with a premium valuation as if they were growing in a healthy way and at an adequate pace. All three companies have a forward P/E above 20x, even with this low growth.

And this is not an exception, several other companies with different profiles also trade at multiples that are not “cheap” and deliver low growth, such as The Hershey Company (HSY) or even Lockheed Martin Corporation (LMT).

Well, as I mentioned, this can also be seen from a positive angle, because even if I disagree, if I allocated 5% of my portfolio to SCHD, I would be forced to hold a small (or tiny) position in each of these companies and consequently be hedged in case the HALO trend continues (heavy assets low obsolescence).

But what cannot be seen as positive is the opportunity cost. Because the ~5% that I would be buying in SCHD, I could be buying other stocks/ETFs that are much more asymmetric. The most obvious is the S&P500.

While SCHD’s profile is, in general, more mature companies that deliver a lot of cash flow but slow growth with a valuation between “fair” and overvalued (of course there are many exceptions), the profile of the S&P500 and Nasdaq are high growth companies with valuations often lower than the companies mentioned.

Just compare the table below with the others. It does not make sense to me to see Microsoft with an estimated growth of 15.6% just for the top line next year, being traded at a P/E practically the same as Coca-Cola. It is also very strange to me to see that Meta has an estimated top line growth above 20%, and the company being literally cheaper than Coca-Cola Company. Of course the risks are different, uncertainties are different, but overall, just as SCHD has the “creme de la creme” of consumer staples, the S&P500 and Nasdaq have the best tech companies.

What I mean by this is that even if uncertainties are there, the asymmetry is even greater. There is an opportunity cost in “passing” on an S&P500 with a very large weight in tech stocks that have very high growth prospects and a valuation well below the average (5y avg forward P/E of MSFT for instance is 31x, a 20% discount) to buy mature companies.

Of course, as I noted in the first section, this does not make SCHD a bad instrument. But I believe these factors need to be analyzed when adding or increasing a SCHD position in the portfolio, the relative valuation versus the main market indexes and this implicit opportunity cost.

Bottom Line Good But I Do Not Want To Buy It Now

I do not see this article as a bearish piece, and I do not think you should see it that way. SCHD’s long-term thesis remains intact, and if I were to give a more specific rating to this ETF, I would say it is a “solid hold”. It operates with a methodology that works as strong diversification and a “bias remover”, buying good companies that pay good dividends even if the market disagrees, but the opposite is also true, at times it will pay a premium for companies that the market is pricing due to some euphoria or a rotation driven by fear in other sectors.

That is what I think is happening now, the market is afraid of a more uncertain scenario in tech, and ends up making traditional SCHD holdings more expensive. Therefore, in the current momentum I prefer to be more selective, either by doing stock picking or preferring to have greater exposure to the S&P500 than to the ETF, which seems to offer a much more attractive and competitive asymmetry.

data.title: Customers Bancorp: As Much As I Want To, I Can't Keep My 'Buy' Rating
data.description:
One of my better calls in recent years has been my bullish stance on Customers Bancorp, Inc. (CUBI). Since I last reaffirmed the company as a "Buy" candidate in early October of last year, the stock has risen 21.6%. That's well above the 5.9% rise that the S&P 500 saw over the same window of time. But that's not all. I first turned bullish on it in August 2023. And in that time, the stock has skyrocketed 126.6%. The market, meanwhile, is up only 61.4% over that same period. This is a monumental amount of appreciation. And it can be attributed to high-quality assets and continued expansion. At some point, however, all good things must come to an end. And it is my view, based on how the company is priced, that taking a slightly more cautious approach is justified. Because of that, I have decided to downgrade it to a "Hold" for now. Though in all honesty, it wouldn't take much for me to upgrade it once again.

Hopefully, that will happen in the coming days. I say that because, on April 24, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. The current expectation is for revenue and profitability to improve. But unless something really big and unexpected occurs that is a positive for shareholders, I still don't see myself upgrading it. Instead, adopting the aforementioned "Neutral" stance seems to make the most sense.

Time To Scale Back

After such an amazing run higher, it shouldn't be surprising that I am downgrading Customers Bancorp from a "Buy" to a "Hold." This does not mean that I think negatively about the quality of the institution or its operational potential moving forward. In fact, in many respects, the company is doing a fine job. At the end of 2025, for instance, deposits came in at $20.78 billion. That's up from the $18.85 billion that the institution had a year earlier. I am a bit frustrated by the fact that 33% of deposits are brokered. This means that they are high-cost deposits borrowed from other financial institutions. But that alone is not enough to justify a downgrade. I do think it's also worth pointing out that uninsured deposit exposure is quite high. At the end of last year, it amounted to $8.6 billion. That was 41.4% of its overall deposit base. In addition to being above the 30% maximum that I typically like to see, it also happened to be higher than the $7.3 billion, or 38.7% of total deposits, that the company had classified as uninsured in 2024.

As deposits increased, the value of loans grew also. These expanded from $14.31 billion in 2024 to $16.60 billion last year. The most significant chunk of its loan portfolio ended up being the specialized lending category that accounted for $7.09 billion. This includes direct finance and sales-type equipment leases, but it mostly consists of investments in fund finance activities, real estate specialty finance, technology and venture finance, healthcare and financial institutions, and municipal finance. This is inherently riskier than most other forms of exposure. And the size of it means that the risk profile could result in damage should loan performance worsen. I'm not saying that will happen, but it is a risk factor.

We would be wise to talk about other aspects of the balance sheet as well. Take, if you will, the value of securities that the company has. The business ended 2025 with $2.67 billion worth of these assets. That was down from the $3.01 billion that the company had a year earlier. Cash and cash equivalents, meanwhile, went from $3.79 billion up to $4.41 billion. And debt went from $1.41 billion to $1.71 billion. When you combine all of these together, what it means is that the company is ratcheting up leverage some to boost cash and continue to invest heavily in growing its loan portfolio. Obviously, the decision to reduce exposure to securities also played a role here.

As the size and the composition of its balance sheet changed, its overall profitability changed also. In the chart above, you can see that for the final quarter of 2025, we saw a nice jump in net interest income. Normally such a large change would imply some one-time event, like, in the previous year, an outsized provision for credit losses. But that's not what we saw. That metric barely budged, inching up from $21.2 million to $22.3 million. The increase in the size of the balance sheet played a large role, but the company also saw a rise in its net interest margin from 3.11% to 3.40%. In the chart below, you can see that and the change in the yield or costs of some of its balance sheet items. Even though certain assets, such as loans and securities, became less profitable for the company during this time, the company was able to drastically reduce how much it was paying depositors and what it was paying on its debt as the Federal Reserve slashed interest rates. On net, this was a positive for the institution, as the data clearly shows. And the same holds true when it comes to 2025 in its entirety compared to 2024.

Even though a declining interest rate environment has proven beneficial for the institution, this does not mean that we can expect that trend to continue. In fact, if management is correct, declining interest rates could have a negative impact on profitability. In the image below, you can see what rate shocks of between 1% and 3%, both in the positive direction and in the negative direction, would do to net interest income. But the good news is that, even with these items, we are not looking at a huge hit to shareholders if interest rates are cut.

Non-interest income has been quite a bit more volatile. Largely because of substantial losses on the sale of investment securities in the final quarter of 2024, the company saw an increase last year in non-interest income. This was instrumental in pushing net profits up from $23.3 million to $70.1 million. And for the year as a whole, the company reported profits of $209.2 million. That stacks up nicely against the $166.4 million that the business reported the previous year.

In the chart above, you can see how the company is priced in relation to earnings. Unfortunately, with a price-to-earnings multiple of 12.7, the bank is more expensive than I would typically like to see. My preference is for a price-to-earnings multiple of 10 or lower. This also happens to put the institution around the middle of the group compared to the five banks that I stacked it up against. In that same chart, you can see that two of the five firms ended up being cheaper than it is in this regard. That ranking is very similar to the ranking that we get when looking at the price-to-book and price-to-tangible-book multiples. In the chart below, you can see that three of the five firms are cheaper than it is on a price-to-book basis. And two of them are cheaper when it comes to the price-to-tangible-book approach.

Normally, I would have downgraded the company earlier. However, the saving grace for the institution leading up to this point has been its overall asset quality. In the first chart below, you can see a return on assets reading of 1.20%. That's above the 1% minimum that I typically look for, and it puts the company above three of the five banks that I stacked it up against. In the subsequent chart below that, I did the same type of analysis using return on equity. With a reading of 13.28%, our prospect is above the 10% minimum that I typically look for. It also puts the business above four of the five banks that I compared it to.

One last thing I would like to touch on would be credit quality. This is distinct from asset quality. In the image below, you can see certain metrics here. Especially interesting would be non-performing assets and non-accrual loans. These numbers are both quite a bit lower than the 0.50% maximum that I typically like to see. And this means that, at least as of yet, there is not any reason to be materially concerned about the institution.

As I mentioned at the start of this article, on April 24, management will be announcing financial results for the first quarter of the company's 2026 fiscal year. Leading up to that point, analysts believe that revenue will come in at about $196 million. That would represent a nice jump over the $143 million reported a year earlier. However, this is a bit deceptive. I say that because, during the first quarter of last year, this revenue figure included a $51.3 million impact associated with losses on investment securities. Without this, we would have seen revenue of about $194.3 million. But I digress. Because of that overall weakness, earnings per share last year ended up being just $0.30, which translated to net profits of $9.5 million. But this year, analysts expect profits of $1.89, which translates to $66.9 million.

Takeaway

Based on the data that's available right now, it is my belief that the easy money has been made when it comes to Customers Bancorp. If we were to annualize the financial performance expected for the first quarter of 2026, we would get down to a price-to-earnings multiple of 9.9. But given the other factors that I have already mentioned and the uncertainty about the future, especially in what I believe will be a worsening economic climate, I believe that downgrading it to a respectful "Hold" makes sense here.

data.title: Fossil: Continued Declining Sales And Macro Headwinds Make Me Bearish
data.description:
The demand for Fossil Group, Inc.'s (FOSL) products has been continuously declining over the past years, and their bottom-line results also consistently came in negative. Although the stock price in the past 12 months increased by more than 500%, I do not think that the firm is fundamentally attractive, and I also do not think that this stock price increase is justifiable fundamentally. Considering the current macroeconomic landscape - including consumer confidence and energy prices - I am even more doubtful. Today, my aim is to give you a balanced view about the firm and highlight why I believe that FOSL is not an attractive stock to own.

Sales

In fiscal 2025, FOSL posted a sales decline of 12.3% compared to the prior year, proprietary and licensed product sales both falling.

If we break down the revenue by geography, we can also see that it is not a single region that is pulling the sales down, but the firm is struggling globally. Demand seems to be exceptionally weak, with sales falling by double digits in 3 out of 4 reported geographic regions.

Further, when we look at the product categories individually, we can also see that the lack of demand seems to be broad-based across all categories as well.

When a firm is struggling to keep customer interest across all categories, they have a major problem, in my view. And this has been the case for years now. Although there are plans of potential turnarounds, I do not see any signs of these plans materialising - at least not on the demand side. I believe that as long as the firm cannot come up with an appealing product, which could help them differentiate themselves from the competition, a successful turnaround is not very likely. Especially when we consider the macroeconomic headwinds as well. For example, consumer confidence in the United States is at historic lows. When people are less certain about their financial stability, they are also less likely to spend on non-essential, discretionary goods - just like the ones FOSL is selling. Even if their products were appealing to the customers, they would still be struggling because of this - in my view.

All in all, I find the continued downward trend of the sales figures disturbing, and I do not see a successful turnaround materializing in the near term, also because of the challenging macro landscape.

Profitability

I was quite bearish on the company from a sales point of view, so it is time for me to be a bit more upbeat and point out some items that may actually make an investment thesis look slightly better.

First of all, the company's gross margin expanded despite the declining gross profit. Gross margin in fiscal 2025 reached 56.1% as a result of less discounting and sourcing initiatives.

When it comes to the operating costs, the firm also appears to be executing its "Transform and Grow" initiative (launched in 2023 for a period of 36 months) relatively well, and there are promising signs - at least in some areas. Sales productivity actually improved, driven by pricing and fewer promotions. Maybe the product assortment also helps, but as long as the demand for these products is low, it is difficult to call the assortment optimal.

Further, the SG&A expenses also decreased both in absolute terms and relative to revenue, driven by the cost reductions and efficiencies as a result of the restructuring programs. This also seems to be in line with the goals highlighted in the second and third points of the Transform and Grow initiative.

Although these results and achievements are attractive in my view, I am not so optimistic about the profitability and the margins going forward - mainly because of the uncertainty around the U.S.-Iran war. Right after the start of the war, energy prices skyrocketed as the Strait of Hormuz was closed. Prices moderated after a ceasefire deal was struck, but peace talks are not going as well as expected, with JD Vance delaying his trip to Pakistan. As long as oil prices are elevated, manufacturing and transportation costs are also expected to be high, which means that if FOSL is not able to pass on these elevated expenses to their customers, their margins are likely going to suffer. I believe that with demand being so weak, it is going to be difficult without losing customers. The demand for Fossil's products is also likely to be very elastic, and the competition in the space is also intense.

Balance Sheet

From a balance sheet side of things, the firm also looks somewhat better. Although their cash reserves are shrinking - as they are generating net losses - they still have more current assets than current liabilities, meaning that from a liquidity point of view, I am not yet concerned. The relatively low levels of debt are also important now, with all the uncertainties around the interest rates. At the beginning of the year, analysts were still expecting rate cuts, but due to the elevated energy prices, the cuts may be delayed, meaning that the cost of debt could stay higher for longer. Fortunately, with relatively low levels of debt, FOSL is well-positioned to withstand these headwinds.

Valuation

In general, I do not like to discuss the valuation of a firm whose fundamentals are continuously deteriorating because even if the price multiples appear attractive, they may be misleading, and it could still be a potential value trap. In FOSL's case, for example, EV/Sales and price/sales multiples indicate an undervaluation compared to the sector median, which may sound promising at first sight, but the fundamentals actually justify the discount.

Regardless of how attractive multiples may be, I do not tend to recommend stocks where the path to profitability is so unclear. While FOSL's losses narrowed over the past three years, it still does not compensate for the fact that the company is still losing money, and it is still eroding shareholder value. If we talked about a high-growth tech company where losses were narrowing, that would be a different discussion, but taking into account how the demand for the company's products is falling continuously, I do not think there is much to be optimistic about. The number of shares outstanding has also been increasing over the past years, meaning that dilution also appears to be an issue.

For these reasons, I do not believe that FOSL should be owned by value investors right now.

Conclusions

Fossil's sales have been declining already for years, and the downward trend does not seem to be stopping. The decline is broad-based, across all product categories and all geographies. The current macroeconomic landscape - especially the low consumer confidence levels - is likely to make the business even more difficult for the firm.

In fiscal 2025, there were slight margin improvements due to effective cost control, but elevated energy prices are likely to lead to increased manufacturing and transportation costs, potentially hurting FOSL's profitability metrics.

The firm appears healthy from a balance sheet point of view, with enough current assets to cover current liabilities. The level of debt also seems to be manageable now, but if the firm continues to generate net losses, they might find themselves in a difficult situation.

Based on the fundamentals, specifically the poor sales figures and continued net losses, I believe that FOSL is not attractive valued - it may appear to be a value trap at best.

For these reasons, I rate FOSL as a sell.

data.title: Kennametal: The Fundamentals Still Aren't Cutting It For Me
data.description:
Shares of Kennametal Inc. (KMT) have recently seen some momentum, which made shares revisit the higher end of their long-term trading range. The fact that the company trades in a long-term range reveals that the long-term track record of Kennametal has been a bit mixed, to put it mildly.

The company sells the current momentum as a more structural upturn, driven by rapidly increasing tungsten prices. However, I fear a notorious past cyclical element of the business. While the business might have improved its quality a bit, I am very fearful to chase the current momentum but willing to monitor the situation from here.

Other interesting growth players tied to the electrical theme are discussed more extensively at Value In Corporate Events.

Solid Momentum

In February, Kennametal reported its second-quarter results for 2026, with second-quarter sales up 10% to $530 million, marking a solid acceleration from the first quarter, with year-to-date revenue up nearly 7%.

The company relies on its operations in the Americas for about half of sales, complemented largely by the EMEA region and, to a lesser extent, activities in Asia Pacific. In terms of actual activity segments, its famous metal-cutting business makes up just over 60% of sales. Second quarter revenue rose over 11% to $331 million, with segment margin reported around 9% of sales. Infrastructure sales rose by just over 7% to $198 million; these, however, are more profitable activities with margins seen near 12%, all ahead of very modest corporate cost allocation.

Quarterly GAAP earnings came in at $0.44 per share, with adjusted earnings per share at $0.47 per share, with both numbers nearly doubling on the year before. The stronger quarter resulted in a hike in the full-year guidance. Sales are now seen between $2.19 and $2.25 billion, with adjusted earnings seen between $2.05 and $2.45 per share. This suggests stronger momentum in the second half of the year and a spectacular improvement from a $1.34 per share number reported for the fiscal year 2025.

Net debt is reported at $469 million. By my math, adjusted EBITDA came in around $283 million in 2025, as nearly a dollar per-share improvement in post-tax earnings this year would imply that pre-tax profits are seen up around a hundred million. That would result in adjusted EBITDA around $375 million by my math, reducing leverage to the low 1s. All this sounds promising, but the quality of margin gains is a bit mixed, largely driven by pricing, timing efforts, and tariff surcharges.

Valuation Perspectives

The strong momentum surprised investors as well, with a stock trading in the low twenties in October, having risen to a high in the low $40s in recent times, now exchanging hands at $38 per share. Based on the midpoint of the adjusted earnings guidance, shares trade at 16-17 times adjusted earnings, which looks cheap, but I have some questions on the earnings power.

For that, we only have to look at the track record, with sales reported just shy of $2 billion in fiscal 2025, being completely flat over the past decade, indicating real declines in sales if we account for inflation. GAAP operating profits have typically been seen in the mid-to-high single digits, and except for a brief episode in the double digits post-pandemic, margins are moving up again here. Amidst a largely flattish share count, although some buybacks have been executed in recent years, all this is far from encouraging, with no real lasting value being created.

That is very much evident in the share price, this being a stock in the $20s in the 1990s already. Shares have largely traded in a $20-$40 range in the decades following, having traded on both the lower end and higher end of the trading range at multiple times.

Not Finding Comfort

Shares of Kennametal have essentially doubled here, with this momentum and anticipated earnings growth driven by higher input prices, which are already passed on to customers in the case of Kennametal, providing a near-term profit boom. This is driven by strong tungsten prices, driven by mining closures, export quotas in China, as well as stockpiling by certain client groups.

Tungsten is key in Kennametal's offerings, with this rare metal having a very high melting point, which provides exceptional hardness, used in high-heat-resistant applications as well as electrical applications.

While this momentum is comforting, at least in the near term, some long-term volume growth might be seen in the industry with electrical applications in greater demand. Yet, I am erring cautiously. The big profit jump anticipated in 2026 is driven by pricing, with underlying volumes seen between flat and positive 3%. Moreover, the long-term performance is very modest, this being a cyclical player with no serial growth seen here.

Hence, I entirely understand that investors are upbeat about the current earnings growth, yet this earnings growth is largely, almost entirely, driven by pricing, which makes me a bit cautious. The real fundamental performance of the business does not seem to be improving that much here. While the idea is "sold" as a structural growth story, I have some concerns here, fearing the cyclical overhang.

data.title: MSCI: Record Sales And High Retention Prove This Compounder Is A Buy
data.description:
MSCI (MSCI) has just released Q1 2026 results, and they were pretty solid: both adjusted EPS and operating revenue topped estimates. The first grew by 14.10%, and the second one increased by 13.80%.

While I am writing, MSCI is approaching $600 per share, about 10% higher than my previous buy rating (October 2025) and outperforming the S&P 500 Index by ~3%. Is the stock still undervalued?

Market Concerns Appear to Be Unfounded

As soon as the Q1 2026 earnings report was released, the stock market reacted positively with a gain of 6%. The reason was that Q1 2026 numbers didn’t objectively show any signs of revenue slowing down.

Over the past few months we have been surrounded by the AI apocalypse narrative that will reshape our world. A lot of untouchable, high-margin companies could be replaced by efficient AI Agents able to replicate their business at a fraction of the costs. These concerns are especially affecting SaaS companies, but they eventually extended to other industries as well, including finance.

In fact, the market started to wonder whether MSCI might be one of the victims of the recent AI developments. After all, one day AI Agents could create their own indices, making MSCI’s most important business totally worthless. In addition, AI Agents could create from scratch new and cheaper risk models than Barra, which means lower recurring operating revenue for MSCI.

This narrative looks scary, and this is the main reason why MSCI is traded at low price multiples compared to the past: the current Price-to-Free Cash Flow multiple is ~12% lower than the 10-year average.

However, since MSCI is not showing deteriorating fundamentals, the market narrative is gradually changing. In this respect, the Q1 2026 earnings report is accelerating MSCI’s comeback. I didn’t find any sign that supports the AI apocalypse narrative.

Starting with the index segment, operating revenues improved by 17.70% compared to Q1 2025, which is totally the opposite of a declining business.

AI is already deeply integrated into our lives, and MSCI’s indexes are still among the best choices in the industry; nobody trusts an AI Agent that creates its own indexes, or at least not yet. The same goes for the Analytics segment, which has grown by 10.30%.

After hundreds of billions of dollars spent by AI hyperscalers over the past few years, I believe that this segment would have suffered at least a little, according to the AI narrative, but this is not what evidence shows us: a declining business doesn’t grow in double digits.

Overall, the organic recurring subscription run rate was 8.20% in Q1 2026, and the retention rate reached 95.40%, even higher than 95.30% in Q1 2025. In other words, MSCI’s services are still highly demanded, and customers can’t do without them; otherwise, the retention rate would have been at least a bit lower compared to the same quarter last year.

Besides the double-digit growth rate, the high profitability remains one of MSCI's biggest strengths. In fact, operating margins reached 53.70% in Q1 2026, 310 basis points higher than the same quarter last year. Regarding guidance, despite the current concerns related to the ongoing energy crisis, management has confirmed the previous estimates.

Finally, shareholders' remuneration is still a core focus for this company. In Q1 2026 and through April 20, MSCI has repurchased 835,591 shares worth $464 million at an average price of $555. Since I believe the company is currently traded at a pretty reasonable price, the buyback has been rather effective.

The company still has $1.70 billion left to complete its share buyback program, and once it is done, the Board will likely authorize a new one. MSCI has historically shown to be a company very focused on reducing total shares outstanding in order to boost EPS and dividends per share. Regarding the latter, the Board declared a $2.05 dividend per share. At first glance it seems irrelevant, but in reality it is not. The $2.05 dividend per share is 14% higher than last year, which is in line with the 3-year dividend CAGR.

Therefore, it is true that the dividend is currently very low (1.45% dividend yield FWD), but the double-digit growth rate makes it a compelling choice for dividend growth investors. If the company increases the dividend by 12% over the next 10 years, your dividend yield on cost would be ~3.60% in 2036. Not bad at all for such a sustainable dividend that has the potential to grow even further 10 years from now. After all, we are talking about a great business that might have just started its ascent. ETFs are a relatively new way of investing, and their popularity has exploded over the last years: MSCI is part of the backbone of this industry.

A Long-Term Buy, but Keep an Eye on the Short Term

If someone asked me where MSCI will be 10 years from now, I would have very few doubts: much higher than $600 per share. I mean, this is a fast-growing business with a 39% net profit margin (5-year average) that tends to significantly reduce shares outstanding and sustain a double-digit dividend CAGR. The AI narrative sounds scary, but numbers are telling us a different story.

In the first quarter MSCI delivered strong financial and operating metrics, including a record asset-based-fee run rate and our best Q1 of recurring net-new subscription sales since 2022. Among product lines and client segments, we posted record levels of Q1 recurring sales in both Index and Analytics, along with our best-ever Q1 recurring net-new sales with hedge funds and with banks and broker – dealers.

Henry A. Fernandez, Chairman and CEO of MSCI

In addition, current price multiples are lower than historical levels, even though the company preserved solid fundamentals over the last 10 years.

If we assume that MSCI is going to increase EPS (starting from FY2025, $17.28) by 12% over the next 10 years and it will be traded at a PE Ratio of 30x in 2036, that’s an annual return on investment of ~10.30%. It might not seem that exciting, but take into account that previous estimates were rather conservative. Over the last 10 years, MSCI managed to increase EPS by 20.60%, and the average PE Ratio has been 43x. Therefore, I see the 10% annual return as the starting point.

However, as with every investment, MSCI presents some risks that you can’t overlook. Above all, I believe the current market risk is the most important one.

As I have expressed in multiple articles on Seeking Alpha, the current US–Iran war is causing an energy crisis that threatens global economic growth. As of now, the market seems to be ignoring this risk (the S&P 500 is at an all-time high) as it is strongly confident that the war will end sometime soon without consequences. I firmly disagree with this narrative, and that might be a short-term problem for a business like MSCI.

The Index segment is by far the most important one in terms of operating revenues (58% of the total), and a stock market decline due to the energy crisis would greatly affect it.

Over the last quarter, most of the Index segment growth stemmed from asset-based fees (26.60% YoY), which is highly influenced by the global stock market performance. The more the stock market rises, the higher the AUM of the ETFs that replicate MSCI’s indexes, which ultimately leads to higher fees for the company. If the stock market drops, the virtuous cycle turns into a vicious one. From this point of view, even though MSCI is a very solid business, it probably won’t help you during a stock market crash. Capital flight is MSCI’s biggest weakness, and nothing can prevent it during a bear market. Therefore, even though I believe it is a great company to hold over the long run, I can’t exclude possible short-term pain.

data.title: Warsh Testimony
data.description:
Kevin Warsh endured more than two hours of testimony this morning.

He wants to be the "chief" of the Federal Reserve.

From what I saw, he did a pretty good job.

I think he is very qualified for the job.

But let me be frank.

Mr. Warsh said that he was concerned about inflation.

Mr. Warsh said that keeping inflation down was the Fed's "job."

I believe that keeping inflation down is the Fed's "job."

Right now, I think that the Federal Reserve needs to set a goal to achieve a "strong" U.S. dollar in foreign exchange markets.

I believe that this would be the foundation of keeping inflation down.

Right now, I think that foreign exchange markets doubt the commitment that the Trump administration has toward keeping inflation down.

Consequently, since Trump became president (January 2025), the value of the U.S. dollar has fallen.

Looking, more specifically, at the relationship between the U.S. dollar and the Euro, we see the same result, except upside/down.

Now, just concentrating on the dollar/euro exchange rate, I must go back into the 2010s to get to a level of exchange that I believe would be more appropriate, would be more consistent with a strong U.S. dollar, one not tied up in an inflationary bundle.

In this last chart, we see the U.S. dollar/Euro exchange rate dropping below 1.10.

For the next two years or so, I believe that moving below a 1.10 exchange rate would be too low and would push the U.S. economy too much right away.

I think that moving down to a 1.10 to 1.12 range would be more workable to bring inflation in the U.S. economy down to a rate of inflation that would be manageable in the short run and serve as a transition level for the near term.

Then, after maintaining this level for a couple of years, the U.S. could move to drop the range down to a 1.00 to 1.10 level.

To me, this would bring the U.S. economy, in the world, down to an adequate growth rate combined with an inflation rate that would be less than 2.0 percent.

I believe that for the U.S. economy to maintain its position in the world and for the U.S. dollar to continue to be the No. 1 currency in the world, the Federal Reserve should work hard to keep the dollar strong.

This would be the combination of results that would make the U.S. the focus of the world and make the president very, very pleased with what has been accomplished under his leadership.

I think Mr. Warsh could get him there.

From what I have seen so far, I believe that Mr. Warsh could get him there.

Do I believe that this will be the direction that is to be achieved?

Not really.

But, we do need to keep thinking about what should be done...and about what should not be done.

Unfortunately, I think the future will see rising inflation.

Fighting this battle against inflation will only be put off until another day, when we have to combat the faster rise in prices.

data.title: Carl Zeiss Meditec: Bad Optics But With 'Buy' Potential
data.description:
Earlier this year, I started the expansion of my coverage of healthcare equipment names. Most readers may know me from coverage of aerospace and defense as well as semiconductors, but there is more overlap with healthcare equipment than many investors think. In this report, I discuss the investment case for Carl Zeiss Meditec AG (CZMWF), which interestingly shows that geopolitics are impacting the business.

High-Tech And Low Error Tolerance Bind Healthcare With Aerospace

While it may not be immediately clear that there is an overlap between aerospace and defense and healthcare equipment names, there actually is a lot of overlap.

In fact, several aerospace and defense companies maintain direct exposure to medical technology markets or leverage similar engineering capabilities across both sectors. From a research perspective, analyzing companies in the healthcare equipment market therefore provides valuable insight into adjacent high-technology markets. This perspective is also reinforced by my academic background. As part of my aerospace and defense education, I pursued a minor track in MedTech-based Entrepreneurship, which focused on the development, commercialization, and financing of medical technologies.

Looking Beyond The Generic Investment Thesis

The generic investment thesis for many manufacturers of healthcare equipment is that life expectancy is increasing and treatment methods and tools are getting more advanced as technology and medical innovation also advance. However, with the complexity, there is also a cost attached, and what we are seeing now with Carl Zeiss Meditec is that sales to China are under pressure and capital expenditures are being pushed out as a function of macro and healthcare budgets. While geopolitics is not necessarily a root cause, it does serve as an amplifier. Tariffs and forex effects can creep up in the supply chain.

China accounts for roughly a quarter of the sales, and that is a state-regulated purchase process, as buying in bulk provides a stronger position to negotiate discounts. When those orders stall due to geopolitical flexing or simply because hospitals are looking to commit to capital expenditures when policies are clearer, it can affect healthcare equipment manufacturers. So, the investment case is not as simple as “people get older and equipment gets more advanced, so the sky is the limit.” There are other factors, such as the timing of orders and macro pressures, that also play a role.

Carl Zeiss Meditec has two segments:

  1. Ophthalmology
    1. Providing imaging systems such as scanners.
    2. Providing surgical systems for laser eye surgery and cataract surgery.
    3. Consumables such as intraocular lenses.
  2. Microsurgery
    1. Providing microscopes used for neurosurgery, ENT (ear, nose, and throat), and spine surgery.

The business has recurring revenue drivers in the form of consumables, service contracts, and software digital platform revenues that layer on top of the revenues from the production of tools and medical devices.

Weak Start Of The Year Triggers Outlook Suspension

In the first quarter, sales declined by 4.8% to €467 million, driven by a 5.1% decrease in Ophthalmology sales to €356.9 million and Microsurgery sales declining 3.7% to €110.1 million. We note that the FX-adjusted revenues for these segments showed declines of 2.4% and 0.9%. So, a substantial portion of the decline was driven by changes in the currency exchange rates. Besides that, the intraocular lenses business was impacted by the withdrawal of bifocal lenses from the volume-based procurement tender in China and the timing of the Chinese New Year delaying treatment sales. The Microsurgery segment saw a reduction in sales driven by the comparable period last year having strong equipment deliveries, which remained absent this year.

Gross margins reduced by 2.8 points to 48.6%, while EBITA tumbled 77% to €8.1 billion, indicating a margin drop of 5.5 points to 1.7%. On an adjusted basis, things did not look much better, with a 69% decline in adjusted EBITA to €10.3 million and margins dropping 4.5 points to 2.2%. So, we are seeing that the company had a weak start to the year, and this was not just driven by China but also by weakness in the overall Asia-Pacific region and North America. The drop was bad enough for Carl Zeiss Meditech to remove its 2025/2026 outlook targeting €2.3 billion in sales with EBITA margins of 12.5%.

Carl Zeiss Meditec Stock Initiated With Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Note: Price targets are based on the ordinary shares traded in Frankfurt under the ticker AFX converted to USD. The stock also is available under the OTC tickers CZMWY and CZMWF.

We note that Carl Zeiss Meditec has lost over 80% over the past five years, and that is driven by the fact that the company used to trade as a medtech premium optics company, but growth fell behind expectations and resulted in a multi-year downtrend. That is also the risk for Carl Zeiss Meditec, but we note that we are now valuing the stock at an EV/EBITDA multiple below that of the peer group and at a third of the company’s longer-term EV/EBITDA. This closely aligns with where the stock price is trading now with 2026 earnings in mind. That would allow for roughly 20% upside with a $39.26 price target when looking at earnings a year ahead.

The CAGR on sales is not impressive at just 3.5%, but EBITDA is expected to show 7% annualized growth and 12% annualized free cash flow growth, with the note that this year will likely largely be a flat year for the company.

If we look at the margin profile, we can see that margins show a bit of a bathtub shape with partial recovery towards 2028. For this year, the expectation is that free cash flow will be more or less stable with growth in the years ahead. Capital intensity is around 40% of operating cash flow in the coming years, with a reduction to 33% by 2028. The company has a €400 million loan from the Zeiss Group, and that is the only meaningful debt it has. So, if Carl Zeiss Meditec executes, we should see significant improvement in the company’s net-debt leverage, which was already not in a horrible position to start with, even more so given that the loan is obtained from the Zeiss Group and likely includes flexible repayment terms.

Conclusion: Carl Zeiss Meditec Is Not A Great Company But Still A "Buy"

Carl Zeiss Meditec is unlikely to be my favorite play for healthcare equipment. The company has high exposure to China, but beyond there is overall hesitance in the Asia-Pacific and North American regions, and Carl Zeiss Meditec simply has not lived up to its premium valuation levels. However, even when priced at a discount to peers, I still see upside, and if the market recovers, I believe the stock could appreciate. As a result, I am marking the stock a "Buy."

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: VBR: Leading Passive Small Value ETF
data.description:
Fast Facts About The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF

The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF (VBR) was launched on January 26, 2004, and tracks the CRSP US Small Cap Value Index. VBR has a portfolio of 838 stocks, a 30-day SEC yield of 2.00%, and an expense ratio of 0.05% (recently down from 0.07%). It is a very large and liquid ETF, with $32.6 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $78 million. Dividends are paid quarterly. It is also available as a mutual fund (VSIAX).

The sponsor, Vanguard Group, was founded in 1975 and has about $12 trillion in total AUM. Vanguard has a unique ownership structure: it is owned by its U.S.-based funds, which are owned by their shareholders.

Strategy

As described by CRSP, the underlying index…

Represents the Value Style for companies covering 85% to 98% of cumulative capitalization of CRSP US Total Market.

Stocks are classified as “Value” based on metrics such as price-to-book, price-to-earnings (historical and forward), price-to-sales ratios, and dividend yield. The index is weighted based on free-float market capitalization and reconstituted on a quarterly basis. The portfolio turnover rate was 16% in the most recent fiscal year. This article will use as a benchmark the parent index CRSP US Small Cap Index, represented by Vanguard Small-Cap ETF (VB).

Portfolio

The fund has significant exposure in industrials (21.6% of asset value), financials (18.2%), and consumer discretionary (14%). Other sectors are below 10%. Compared to the parent index, VBR mostly overweights financials and downplays technology, healthcare, and telecommunications.

The fund has very low company-specific risk. The top 10 holdings are listed below and only represent 6.1% of asset value, with the heaviest position weighing 0.75%.

Although VBR has a low yield and is not designed to attract dividend investors, it boasts strong dividend growth. The annual sum of distributions has increased by 111% between 2015 and 2025, greatly outpacing cumulative inflation (about 38% in the same time, based on CPI).

Competitors

The next table compares characteristics of VBR and four of the largest U.S. small-cap value ETFs:

VBR is the largest of these funds and has the lowest expense ratio. It comes in third position for dollar trading volumes, a clue that it attracts more long-term investors than traders. Based on total return and Sharpe ratio since June 2021, VBR is the best performer among the passively managed ETFs on this list. It also shows the lowest risk metrics (volatility and drawdown). However, it significantly underperforms the active funds AVUV and DFAT. These are not strictly single-factor value ETFs, as they also use profitability metrics in their selection process.

Takeaway

The Vanguard Small-Cap Value Index Fund ETF has a portfolio of over 800 small U.S. companies selected based on value metrics, with sector diversification and very low company risk. VBR is well-suited for investors seeking low-cost exposure to the value factor in small caps for long-term or tactical allocation. Nonetheless, some active small-cap ETFs, such as AVUV and DFAT, offer more attractive returns by blending value and quality.

  • Pros: Low cost, low volatility, diversification, outperforms passive ETF peers.
  • Cons: Lags some active ETFs.

This article answers these three main questions about VBR:

  1. How diversified is the VBR portfolio?
  2. How does VBR compare to benchmarks and peers?
  3. What type of strategy is VBR best suited for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: GSST: Goldman Fund Delivering On Its Promises
data.description:
Thesis

It has been a year since we last covered the Goldman Sachs Ultra Short Bond ETF (GSST), and we are going to do an annual review on the name and its rating. The ETF falls in the short-term bond fund bucket and represents a way to park cash while clipping an enhanced yield when compared to treasuries. In today's article we are going to focus on whether GSST has been able to achieve that and if investors are well served to put their capital to work via GSST.

What Do Short-Term Bond Funds Do?

Short-term bond funds exist in order to enhance yields while keeping risks contained. Investors have realized they can get anywhere from 20 bps to 80 bps over T-Bills by parking cash in short-term bond funds, figures which are becoming more important as rates move lower. One of the key features is the risks taken and the volatility posted. Some funds take more credit risk, while others take more duration risk to generate additional yield. The best-run funds take a little of both but, more importantly, are really, really good at managing the downside (i.e., the risk profile) for such a fund.

As Rates Move Lower, Short-Term Bond Funds Are Becoming More Attractive

While the market can debate ad nauseam what the Fed will do in 2026, it should be clear by now from the Fed dot plot that we are in a monetary easing environment where rates are headed lower:

How low they will go is a matter of debate, but when looking at the above chart, we can see a potential neutral rate at 3%. In a net low-rate environment, 50 bps becomes much more important than the same 50 bps when Fed Funds are at 6%, for example. Hence the appeal of short-term bond funds.

Also, please remember the political aspect embedded in today's environment, with President Trump being very clear that he is expecting much lower Fed Funds:

Earlier this week, Trump repeated his assertion that interest rates should be much lower than the current range of 3.5% to 3.75%. Asked during an interview on Fox Business Network if he thought rates would go lower later this year, Trump replied: "When Kevin gets in, I do."

If the Iran war is solved quickly, the market will shift back expectations to what we saw in the beginning of the year, which is 1 or 2 Fed cuts in 2026. The political will is clearly tilted towards more cuts, but it is unclear how the new Fed chair will be able to implement that view.

As Fed Funds move lower, T-Bills will continue to deliver smaller and smaller returns, thus making short-dated bond funds with excess spreads more attractive to investors.

GSST Has Been Able to Deliver a Low-Volatility Profile

For us, one of the most important factors when looking at a fund like GSST is its ability to deliver on a low-risk requirement. We do not like funds which have a high standard deviation because they no longer serve the purpose of value preservation but rather act as risk-taking instruments.

When looking at GSST, we can see the ETF posting very low volatility figures:

As per the Seeking Alpha 'Risk' tab, the ETF's standard deviation is a mere 0.69%, while the annualized volatility is 0.6%. As a comparison point, the iShares 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV) has a standard deviation of 0.44% and an annualized volatility of 0.2%. GSST, therefore, has delivered on a low volatility mandate.

GSST Has Delivered on an Excess Spread as Well

When looking at total returns, we also want to make sure that GSST has been able to deliver on its excess spread mandate:

When comparing GSST to purely treasuries or T-Bill funds, we can see a massive outperformance. In the past year, GSST has posted a total return of 4.77%, all while SGOV posted 4.03% and BIL posted 3.94%. The Goldman fund, therefore, has provided roughly 75 to 80 bps of excess return over the competition with minimal volatility impact. This is a massive result, and it speaks volumes to the ability of the fund managers to take smart risks and adjust exposures.

Furthermore, when looking at the above chart, we see exactly how a short-dated bond fund should perform, consistently outperforming its peers with a very low volatility profile. If, for example, GSST would have had a poor build, its drawdown during the most recent market sell-off would have been much greater.

Current Fund Composition

Currently the ETF's composition is as follows:

The name currently clips its excess spread via a large investment-grade corporate bucket, which sits at 55%. The next largest segment is ABS at 14.2%, followed by commercial paper at 9.3%.

The overall portfolio duration is a mere 0.5 years, highlighting that the name does not take significant credit or rates risk. Additional fund analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $1.47 billion
  • Expense ratio: 0.16%
  • SEC yield: 4.1%

From a ratings perspective, the fund is exclusively investment grade, as expected:

GSST contains a large single-A sleeve and only 21% BBB names (the riskiest investment-grade bonds).

Risk Factors

The largest risk factor for this name is a severe and prolonged recession, similar to 2008. While the fund duration is very, very low, credit events do happen during massive recessions. For example, in 2008, the investment-grade-rated Lehman Brothers went into bankruptcy. Even in that scenario, the fund should be able to manage any potential defaults via its granular approach to its build, but it nonetheless does represent a risk factor. From an individual corporate issuer perspective, no issuer represents more than 1% of the fund, meaning that an issuer default would only cause the fund's excess spread to move lower by 1%. This is a very outside scenario, but the main risk factor to consider.

Conclusion

GSST is a short-dated bond fund from Goldman. The name has done an outstanding job since its inception in providing for excess spreads over treasuries while maintaining a low volatility profile. GSST and T Rowe Price Ultra Short-Term Bond ETF (TBUX) are our go-to names for cash parking and clipping a higher yield while waiting for attractive investment opportunities.
